*: A charm of goldfinches

The American '''Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] ([[Fringillidae]]) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the United States north of the Gulf States. It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the [[United States]].

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of peanuts.

http://www.cws-scf.ec.gc.ca/hww-fap/agfinch/agfinch.html

The American '''Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] ([[Fringillidae]]) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the United States north of the Gulf States. It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the [[United States]].

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail.  Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of peanuts.  Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S. are thistle and teasel seeds).

It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] States. It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] ([[Fringillidae]]) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]]. This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]]. This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of the Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
It prefers trees in open places, especially in orchards and along roadsides. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

== Diet ==
Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

== Diet ==
Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

== Diet ==
Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

== Diet ==
Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and sometimes insects. In winter, they are often seen in flocks.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s.  

== Diet ==
American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

In winter, they are often seen in flocks. Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

== Habitat ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Appearance ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Behavior ==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. 
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Diet ==
American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

In winter, they are often seen in flocks. Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

==  Distribution and Migration ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Physical Description and Taxonomy ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Ecology and behaviour==
Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

In winter, they are often seen in flocks. Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.

== Reproduction ==
Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. 
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Conservation status ==
American goldfinches are not threatened, and are widespread throughout their geographic range. However, their numbers have been decreasing during recent decades mostly due to [[habitat loss]] through the commercial development of meadows and flood plains.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

==  Distribution and Migration ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Physical Description and Taxonomy ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]] 

== Ecology and behaviour==
They are migratory birds. They breed in loose colonies, but are social during the nonbreeding season when they are often found in large flocks, usually with other finches.Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

Their song is a series of musical warbles and twitters, often with a long note. A "tsee-tsi-tsi-tsit," call is often given in flight; it may also be described as "per-chic-o-ree" or "po-ta-to-chip."

American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.


== Reproduction ==
Their courtship rituals include daring maneuvers and singing by males. Males court when their bright plumage appears. Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. Most mating pairs raise only one brood each year.
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Conservation status ==
American goldfinches are not threatened, and are widespread throughout their geographic range. However, their numbers have been decreasing during recent decades mostly due to [[habitat loss]] through the commercial development of meadows and flood plains.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

:''See also'': [[European Goldfinch]]

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

==  Distribution and Migration ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Physical Description and Taxonomy ==
In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. The male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap; he has a very pleasing call song. 

In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  

In some winter ranges the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing.

[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]]

== Ecology and behaviour==
They are migratory birds. They breed in loose colonies, but are social during the nonbreeding season when they are often found in large flocks, usually with other finches.Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

Their song is a series of musical warbles and twitters, often with a long note. A "tsee-tsi-tsi-tsit," call is often given in flight; it may also be described as "per-chic-o-ree" or "po-ta-to-chip."

American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.


== Reproduction ==
Their courtship rituals include daring maneuvers and singing by males. Males court when their bright plumage appears. Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. Most mating pairs raise only one brood each year.
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Conservation status ==
American goldfinches are not threatened, and are widespread throughout their geographic range. However, their numbers have been decreasing during recent decades mostly due to [[habitat loss]] through the commercial development of meadows and flood plains.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family, averaging 11 cm in length. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

==  Distribution and Migration ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Physical Description and Taxonomy ==

The American Goldfinch is a small [[finch]], 11-13 cm (4-5 in) in length, with a [[wingspan]] of 19-22 cm (7-9 in). It weighs from 11 to 20 g (0.39-0.71 ounces). 

The bill is small and pointed, conical in shape, and pink during most of the year, though it turns to a bright orange with the spring moult.

The body is a bright yellow to dull, olive brown, the wings are dark, with large white [[wingbars]], and the tail is short and notched. In spring the birds moult all but their black wing and tail feathers, and the bills of both sexes turn orange. 
The breeding male assumes brilliant canary yellow plumage and a striking jet black cap, while females remain a dull olive brown. 

In some winter ranges, the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing. The common winter plumage is a duller olive-brown with some yellow still showing on the head.  
   
In flight, a white rump contrasts with the black tail. 



[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]]

== Ecology and behaviour==
They are migratory birds. They breed in loose colonies, but are social during the nonbreeding season when they are often found in large flocks, usually with other finches.Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

Their song is a series of musical warbles and twitters, often with a long note. A "tsee-tsi-tsi-tsit," call is often given in flight; it may also be described as "per-chic-o-ree" or "po-ta-to-chip."

American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.


== Reproduction ==
Their courtship rituals include daring maneuvers and singing by males. Males court when their bright plumage appears. Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. Most mating pairs raise only one brood each year.
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Conservation status ==
American goldfinches are not threatened, and are widespread throughout their geographic range. However, their numbers have been decreasing during recent decades mostly due to [[habitat loss]] through the commercial development of meadows and flood plains.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.

The '''Eastern''' or '''American Goldfinch''' (''Carduelis tristis'') is a typical North American seed-eating member of the [[finch]] (Fringillidae) family. It breeds across southern [[Canada]] from [[British Columbia]] to [[Newfoundland]] and through most of the [[United States]] north of the [[Gulf of Mexico|Gulf]] and core [[Southwestern United States|Southwestern States]]. 

==  Distribution and Migration ==
The American goldfinch prefers open fields and meadows, flood plains, roadsides, orchards, and gardens. This habitat preference is maintained in the spring and fall migration. 

In winter in the northern part of their range, the finches may move nearer to feeders if they available. As winter approaches, the goldfinch moves short distances towards the south. Its winter range includes southern British Columbia, [[Manitoba]], [[Ontario]], [[New Brunswick]], [[Nova Scotia]], and most of the United States.

== Physical Description and Taxonomy ==

The American Goldfinch is a small [[finch]], 11-13 cm (4-5 in) in length, with a [[wingspan]] of 19-22 cm (7-9 in). It weighs from 11 to 20 g (0.39-0.71 ounces). 

The bill is small and pointed, conical in shape, and pink during most of the year, though it turns to a bright orange with the spring moult.

In spring, the bird undergoes a complete [[moult]], shedding all but its black wing and tail feathers. Once this moult is completed, the body of the male is a brilliant canary yellow with a striking jet black cap, and a white rump that is displayed during flight. The female is an olive yellow, with a yellow bib on her neck and breast. The bills of both sexes turn bright orange after the first moult.

In autumn, they undergo a second moult, replacing the bright feathers of the summer with duller plumage. The autumn plumage is almost identical in colour for both sexes. They are buff below and olive-brown above, with a pale yellow face and bib.The only markings which differentiate the sexes are the [[lesser coverts|shoulder patches]] of the male, which are yellow. In some winter ranges, the birds lose all traces of yellow, becoming predominantly medium tan-grey banded colour, with an olive cast evident only on close viewing. 

In both moults, the wings are dark olive in females and black in males, with large white wingbars, and the dark tail is short and notched, the feathers edged with white.

During their first autumn and winter, the young are medium brown above, with buff-colored, rather than white, wing markings, and dull black shoulders.





[[Image:American goldfinch winter f.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Female American goldfinch in winter plumage.]]

== Ecology and behaviour==
They are migratory birds. They breed in loose colonies, but are social during the nonbreeding season when they are often found in large flocks, usually with other finches.Their flight path is not straight horizontally; instead, they generally fly while going slightly up and down, making an ocean wave-shaped path. 

Their song is a series of musical warbles and twitters, often with a long note. A "tsee-tsi-tsi-tsit," call is often given in flight; it may also be described as "per-chic-o-ree" or "po-ta-to-chip."

American Goldfinches are daytime feeders. Their [[diet]] consists of the seeds from a wide variety of [[annual]] plants, often "weed" seeds, such as the seeds of grasses and trees. Their favorite foods (at least in the U.S.) are [[thistle]] and [[teasel]] seeds. However, they also eat small seeds from other weeds, grasses and trees, tree buds, maple sap and rarely [[insects]], though they feed insects to their young for the [[protein]] content. 

Goldfinches have adapted feeding while hanging on seed heads, which they prefer to ground feeding. The goldfinch drinks taking a beakful of water, then tipping its head back in order to swallow.


== Reproduction ==
Their courtship rituals include daring maneuvers and singing by males. Males court when their bright plumage appears. Breeding pairs form during the winter months, but nesting does not begin until late June or early July. The female constructs the nest, which is cup-shaped and less than 1-10 m (1-30 ft) above ground in a shrubby plant or coniferous tree. The nest building lasts approximately six days. 

The American Goldfinch lays four to six bluish-white eggs, roughly the size of [[peanut]]s. It is thought that they are laid during the night. The female incubates the eggs, while her mate feeds her. Most mating pairs raise only one brood each year.
 
The young hatch about 12 to 14 days after incubation begins. They are born naked, with reddish bodies and eyes closed. The mother bird feeds the young regurgitated seeds and insects as they grow. They develop quickly, first opening their eyes after three days, and [[fledge]] after a week, though the adults continue to feed them for up to three weeks.

== Conservation status ==
American goldfinches are not threatened, and are widespread throughout their geographic range. However, their numbers have been decreasing during recent decades mostly due to [[habitat loss]] through the commercial development of meadows and flood plains.

== Trivia ==
This is the state bird of [[Iowa]], [[New Jersey]], and [[Washington]].<ref>{{cite web | title = Symbols of Washington State | publisher = Washington State Legislature | url = http://www1.leg.wa.gov/Legislature/StateSymbols/ | accessdate = 2007-03-11}}</ref> 

This bird is also known as the '''Wild Canary'''.
